full.stage1:
```json
{
  "bottleneck_type": "memory-bound",
  "data_dependencies": [
    "The computation of B[i][j] depends on A[i][j], A[i][j-1], A[i][j+1], A[i+1][j], and A[i-1][j].",
    "The computation of A[i][j] in the next step depends on B[i][j], B[i][j-1], B[i][j+1], B[i+1][j], and B[i-1][j]."
  ],
  "parallelization_challenges": [
    "The kernel exhibits a stencil pattern, which has inherent data dependencies between neighboring elements.",
    "Boundary conditions need to be handled carefully to avoid out-of-bounds memory access.",
    "The two-step update process (first updating B, then A) requires synchronization between steps."
  ],
  "recommended_strategy": "Use a 2D grid of threads where each thread computes one element of B or A. Employ shared memory to cache neighboring elements to reduce global memory accesses. Synchronize threads at the end of each time step to ensure all updates to B are completed before updating A.",
  "gpu_suitable": true
}
```
full.stage2:
```json
{
  "validation": "partial",
  "corrected_analysis": {
    "bottleneck_type": "memory-bound (strongly), unless n is tiny",
    "why_memory_bound": "Per interior point update: ~5 global loads + 1 global store of doubles (48 bytes) for ~6 FLOPs (5 adds + 1 mul) => ~0.125 FLOP/byte, far below typical GPU balance. Even with some cache reuse, bandwidth dominates.",
    "data_dependencies": [
      "Within a single sweep A->B: B[i][j] depends only on A at (i,j) and its 4-neighbors. There are no write-after-read hazards within the same sweep because B is written once per point and reads only A.",
      "Within a single sweep B->A: A[i][j] depends only on B at (i,j) and its 4-neighbors. Same property.",
      "Across time steps: A at time t+1 depends on A at time t through the intermediate B. This creates a global synchronization requirement between the two sweeps and between time steps."
    ],
    "parallelization_challenges": [
      "No intra-sweep dependencies, so the stencil itself is not a parallelization blocker; the real dependency is the required global barrier between sweeps (A->B then B->A) and between time steps.",
      "Boundary handling (i=0,n-1 or j=0,n-1) must avoid OOB and preserve boundary values (typically unchanged).",
      "Memory traffic is high; naive implementation will be bandwidth-limited. Need to maximize coalescing and reuse via tiling/shared memory (or rely on L2/texture cache if acceptable)."
    ],
    "what_was_incomplete_or_wrong": [
      "Saying 'stencil has inherent data dependencies between neighboring elements' is misleading for Jacobi: neighbors are read-only from the previous array, so all interior points in a sweep are embarrassingly parallel.",
      "Synchronizing 'threads at the end of each time step' is not sufficient on GPU: you need a grid-wide barrier, which typically means separate kernel launches (or cooperative groups with restrictions)."
    ]
  },
  "gpu_strategy": {
    "approach": "Two-kernel-per-timestep Jacobi with 2D tiling in shared memory (halo), optional kernel fusion via cooperative groups if supported; otherwise standard launches. Use ping-pong pointers to avoid extra copies.",
    "description": [
      "Launch kernel1 to compute B = stencil(A).",
      "Launch kernel2 to compute A = stencil(B).",
      "Repeat for tsteps. Each kernel uses a 2D thread block that loads a tile of the input array into shared memory including a 1-cell halo, then computes one output per thread for interior points of the tile.",
      "This reduces redundant global loads of neighbor elements and ensures coalesced accesses along j (row-major)."
    ],
    "kernel_design": {
      "thread_mapping": "2D grid. thread (tx,ty) computes global (j,i) = (blockIdx.x*BX+tx, blockIdx.y*BY+ty). Prefer x dimension mapped to contiguous j for coalescing.",
      "block_size": "Typical: BX=32, BY=8 or 16 (tune). Shared tile size = (BY+2) x (BX+2) doubles.",
      "shared_memory_tiling": [
        "Allocate shared double tile[BY+2][BX+2].",
        "Each thread loads its center element tile[ty+1][tx+1] from global if in-bounds.",
        "Halo loads: threads on block edges additionally load left/right/top/bottom halo cells; optionally corners by 4 threads.",
        "__syncthreads()",
        "Compute output for interior global points (i in 1..n-2, j in 1..n-2) using shared neighbors.",
        "Write output to global."
      ],
      "boundary_handling": "Guard computation with if (i>0 && i<n-1 && j>0 && j<n-1). For boundaries, either skip (leaving pre-initialized values) or explicitly copy input to output if required by the application.",
      "synchronization": "Use kernel launch boundaries as global barriers between A->B and B->A. No device-wide sync inside a normal kernel.",
      "memory_access": "Ensure A and B are contiguous row-major (C layout). Use leading dimension = n. Consider using __ldg()/read-only cache on older CUDA, or rely on modern caching.",
      "precision": "double is bandwidth-heavy; if acceptable, consider float for higher throughput."
    }
  },
  "expected_speedup": "Typically large vs single-thread CPU (10x-100x depending on n, tsteps, GPU/CPU). Versus optimized multicore CPU, often ~2x-10x. Actual speedup is bounded by memory bandwidth; shared-memory tiling helps but may be limited by L2 reuse already capturing some neighbor reuse.",
  "implementation_notes": [
    "Use two device arrays dA and dB; for each timestep launch stencil(dA->dB) then stencil(dB->dA). This is already 'ping-pong'; no extra swap needed if you alternate pointers or just overwrite as shown.",
    "Choose grid dims: dim3 block(BX,BY); dim3 grid(ceil(n/BX), ceil(n/BY)). Computation guards handle edges.",
    "Shared memory footprint: (BX+2)*(BY+2)*8 bytes. Example BX=32,BY=8 => 34*10*8=2720 bytes/block (small).",
    "Unroll halo loads carefully to avoid branch divergence; a common pattern is: load center for all threads, then conditional loads for halos based on tx/ty.",
    "If tsteps is large, kernel launch overhead is usually negligible; if tsteps is small and n is small, overhead can dominate.",
    "Optional advanced: time-tiling (multiple timesteps per launch) is non-trivial for Jacobi because it needs two arrays and introduces larger halos; consider only if tsteps is huge and you can afford complexity.",
    "If using CUDA Cooperative Groups for a single fused kernel with grid-wide sync between the two sweeps, you must launch as a cooperative kernel and ensure the whole grid fits concurrently on the GPU; otherwise prefer two kernels.",
    "For best bandwidth: align allocations, use pitched allocation if helpful, and ensure j is the fastest-varying index in threadIdx.x."
  ]
}
```
